summary:
The Lament for the Destruction of Ur is a Sumerian literary composition written shortly after the fall of the [[event:1724|Third Dynasty of Ur]] around 2004 BC. The text, preserved on multiple clay tablets, describes the devastation wrought upon the city of Ur by the Elamites and Amorites, who sacked the city and brought the Ur III state to an end. Structured as a liturgical lamentation addressed to the gods, the text depicts the goddess Ningal pleading with the divine assembly to spare her city, and then mourning its destruction after the gods grant permission for Ur's ruin.

The Lament for Ur belongs to a genre of Mesopotamian city laments that used the voice of a divine patron to express collective grief over political catastrophe. The genre served both a ritual and a political purpose: the laments were likely performed in religious contexts to justify the catastrophe theologically — presenting the destruction as divinely sanctioned — while also preserving a community's memory of loss. The text remains one of the most emotionally vivid pieces of ancient Near Eastern literature, offering historians rare insight into how Bronze Age communities processed the trauma of political collapse.
